text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

21.1	The Economic Commission for Latin America and the Caribbean (ECLAC) is responsible for fostering the economic, social and environmentally sustainable development of Latin America and the Caribbean through international cooperation, by undertaking applied research and evidence-based comparative analysis of development processes, by providing normative and operational capacity development, and by providing capacity-building, technical cooperation and advisory services in support of regional development efforts. The mandate derives from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly resolutions and decisions, as well as Economic and Social Council resolution 106 (VI), by which the Council established the Commission for the purpose of contributing to and coordinating action towards the economic and social development of the region and reinforcing economic relationships among the countries of Latin America and the Caribbean and with other regions of the world. In 1996, pursuant to ECLAC resolution 553 (XXVI), the Commission decided, inter alia, to collaborate with member States in the comprehensive analysis of development processes geared towards the design, monitoring and evaluation of public policies and the resulting provision of operational services in the fields of specialized information, advisory services, training and support for regional and international cooperation and coordination. The support delivered by the Commission to member States is also provided through the regular programme of technical cooperation and Development Account projects. In Assembly resolution 79/323 on the Sevilla Commitment, Member States reaffirmed their commitment to sustainable development, including effectively implementing the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and its 17 Sustainable Development Goals. The Commission remains uniquely positioned to support its member States in this regard.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

21.2	The Commission will continue to support the development pillar of the United Nations, to foster economic integration at the subregional and regional levels, to support member States in the implementation of the 2030 Agenda and the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals, the Pact for the Future, the Global Digital Compact and the international agreements stemming from the major summits and conferences in the development field, as well as to facilitate sustainable development by helping to bridge economic, social and environmental gaps within and among countries of the region and with other regions of the world. To that end, ECLAC will undertake applied research and evidence-based comparative analysis of development processes and provide normative and operational capacity-building, technical cooperation and advisory services in support of regional development efforts.

21.3	The Commission’s overall strategy for achieving the objectives of the programme is structured around 13 interdependent and complementary subprogrammes that are to be implemented using a multidisciplinary and integrated approach. The present proposed programme budget is also aimed at being responsive to the priorities, policy needs and emerging challenges of member States against a backdrop of increasing challenges and uncertainties.

21.4	To make progress towards more productive, inclusive and sustainable development and address the challenges faced by Latin America and the Caribbean, countries of the region would need to overcome three development traps: (a) low capacity for growth; (b) high inequality, low social mobility and weak social cohesion; and (c) weak institutional capacities and ineffective governance. For the region to achieve stronger growth, productive development policies for growth, investment, employment, productivity and more ample policy space are necessary, as well as strengthened governance and technical, operational, political and prospective capabilities of institutions. At the same time, evolving globalization trends and technological changes are transforming the production paradigm and impacting the implementation of the 2030 Agenda and the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals in Latin American and Caribbean countries. A more ambitious approach can help to drive the structural transformations required to sustain the path towards the fulfilment of the 2030 Agenda and the Goals. To accelerate progress towards achieving the Goals, ECLAC will use key entry points, such as productive transformation policies, food systems, energy access and affordability, digital connectivity, education, jobs and social protection, and climate change, biodiversity loss and pollution.

21.5	At the fortieth session of the Commission, held in Lima in 2024, ECLAC presented to its member States the document Development Traps in Latin America and the Caribbean: Vital Transformations and How to Manage Them, in which it put forward a new proposal to analyse a number of transformations that can assist in the move towards a new development model, including: (a) productive transformation for higher, sustained, inclusive and sustainable growth; (b) transformation to reduce inequality and foster inclusion and social mobility; and (c) transformation to boost sustainability and combat climate change. The document includes various proposals for carrying out these transformations, taking into account developments in the global economy.

21.6	ECLAC will continue to offer its value proposition to member States by combining its think-tank and thought leadership role, its convening role through its intergovernmental platforms and subsidiary bodies, and its operational role by providing technical cooperation, peer learning, and advisory and capacity-building services, anchored in data and evidence-based analysis and understanding of the realities of the region. The Commission will continue to foster a multisectoral approach to development and to give a voice to countries in special situations, such as the one least developed country in the region, landlocked developing countries and Caribbean small island developing States and middle-income countries.

21.7	The Commission will continue to offer its intergovernmental architecture for consensus-building and regional dialogues, to analyse the key means of implementation of the 2030 Agenda and promote the exchange of best practices and lessons learned in its role as technical secretariat for the Committee of the Whole of ECLAC, the Caribbean Development and Cooperation Committee, the Regional Conference on Women in Latin America and the Caribbean, the Regional Council for Planning of the Latin American and Caribbean Institute for Economic and Social Planning, the Statistical Conference of the Americas of ECLAC, the Regional Conference on Population and Development in Latin America and the Caribbean, the Conference on Science, Innovation and Information and Communications Technologies, the Regional Conference on South-South Cooperation in Latin America and the Caribbean and the Regional Conference on Social Development in Latin America and the Caribbean, in addition to the Conference of the Parties to the Regional Agreement on Access to Information, Public Participation and Justice in Environmental Matters in Latin America and the Caribbean (Escazú Agreement), the Forum of Ministers and High-level Authorities on Housing and Urban Development of Latin America and the Caribbean, the Regional Intergovernmental Conference on Ageing and the Rights of Older Persons in Latin America and the Caribbean and the Ministerial Conference on the Information Society in Latin America and the Caribbean.

21.8	Furthermore, ECLAC will continue to provide integrated and comprehensive follow-up to world summits from a regional perspective and coordinate the region’s approach to the implementation of the 2030 Agenda. It will also continue to act as the secretariat of the Forum of the Countries of Latin America and the Caribbean on Sustainable Development, supporting the follow-up and review process of the implementation of the 2030 Agenda at the regional level, and the reporting by the Forum to the high-level political forum on sustainable development at the global level, thereby linking the national, regional and global dimensions of the process.

21.9	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, ECLAC will continue to work closely with the Governments of the region and other stakeholders, including the business community, academia and civil society, to reinforce links between national experiences and regional perspectives, comparability and exchange of good practices, and to provide effective technical cooperation services for transboundary issues. Furthermore, the Commission will continue its close coordination and collaboration with United Nations agencies, funds and programmes in the region and with the resident coordinator system, including through the Regional Collaborative Platform.

21.10	Collaboration will also continue with the Bretton Woods institutions, in particular the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank, and with other relevant organizations, such as the Organization of American States, the Inter-American Development Bank, the Ibero-American General Secretariat and the Development Bank of Latin America and the Caribbean. ECLAC will reinforce its close cooperation with various subregional integration mechanisms and strengthen its collaboration with private sector associations, non-governmental and civil society organizations, think tanks and academia to encourage policy dialogue and engagement in the implementation of the 2030 Agenda, which is driven primarily by member States. The Commission will continue its active participation to foster substantive global coordination among all participating entities in the United Nations Sustainable Development Group.

21.11	ECLAC will continue to enhance its bilateral and multilateral collaboration with partners within and outside the United Nations system, to strengthen its strategic partnerships and mobilize additional extrabudgetary resources to enhance the implementation of the programme of work and maximize its impact in beneficiary countries and at the regional level. The Commission will consolidate development partners’ trust and confidence by undertaking strategic dialogues with them on a regular basis, ensuring the efficient delivery of mandates, showing concrete achievements and providing comprehensive information on the use of their contributions.
21.12	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:
(a)	The international community continues to be committed to implementing the 2030 Agenda and achieving the Sustainable Development Goals;

(b)	The countries of Latin America and the Caribbean continue to support and attach high priority to multilateralism, regional and subregional cooperation and the gradual convergence of integration schemes with a view to broadening the opportunities for achieving economic, social and environmentally sustainable development;
(c)	The regional and international communities support the member States of ECLAC and respond to their specific needs and concerns.

21.13	The Commission integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate, and will continue to ensure that gender focal points (see General Assembly resolution 80/186) are consulted in programme formulation and that gender analysis (see Assembly resolution 79/223) is integrated into all areas under the Commission’s mandate.

21.14	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy and the ECLAC disability inclusion strategy (CEPALDIS), the Commission will continue to implement inclusive and comprehensive measures regarding accessibility, security and improvements in audiovisual technology in conference rooms, and reasonable adaptation of office spaces and furniture, to facilitate the full participation of persons with disabilities in the daily work of the Commission, with a view to further advancing their inclusion.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

21.15	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated planned deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026.

21.16	ECLAC will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals for strengthening collaboration with the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development, the Department of Economic and Social Affairs of the Secretariat and the other regional commissions through joint mechanisms. ECLAC will jointly explore options for strengthening macroeconomic analysis with a view to contributing more harmonized analysis and policy support to Member States, while taking into consideration the specificities of Latin America and the Caribbean in order to tailor the support requested to the concrete needs and demands of the countries of the region.

children.text: Evaluation activities
21.19	The following evaluations completed by ECLAC in 2025 have guided the proposed programme plan for 2027:
(a)	Evaluations of projects on road safety in Colombia, and the participation of small and medium-sized enterprises in e-commerce (subprogramme 1);
(b)	Evaluation of a project on potable water, sanitation and renewable energies in municipalities (subprogramme 8);
(c)	Evaluation of a project on enhancing access to information, public participation and justice in environmental matters in Latin America and the Caribbean (subprogramme 7).

21.20	In response to the results of the evaluations referenced above, ECLAC will strengthen the mainstreaming of the inclusion and participation of vulnerable groups at the onset of project and activity design throughout its programme of work.
21.21	An evaluation of a project on resilient health systems with universal coverage in Latin America and the Caribbean to be conducted by ECLAC is planned for 2027.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – International trade, integration and infrastructure
Objective

21.22	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to strengthen the role of Latin America and the Caribbean in international trade and the global economy by deepening regional integration and enhancing logistics and infrastructure as a means to foster sustainable development.
Strategy
21.23	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Propose adjustments to national and regional trade policies to address emerging challenges and harness new opportunities regarding innovation and technological progress; export diversification opportunities; cross-border digital trade; trade in services; global and regional value chains and internationalization of small and medium-sized enterprises; new public and private standards; gender mainstreaming; environmental sustainability; and links with other emerging issues;

(b)	Evaluate the negotiation, implementation and administration of trade agreements at the bilateral, regional and multilateral levels, with a focus on regional integration initiatives and trade relations with other regions;
(c)	Advocate for export diversification, enhancement of inter-agency coordination and public-private cooperation, institutional modernization and trade facilitation;

(d)	Foster integrated logistics and transport policies and their coordination at the subregional level, considering regional integration initiatives, including the sharing of good practices and lessons learned, to address the infrastructure investment gap in the region;
(e)	Analyse logistics, trade facilitation and infrastructure development at the national and transboundary levels, in particular in landlocked developing countries and countries with special needs.
21.24	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	National policies that promote more sustained, inclusive and sustainable economic growth through sustainable consumption and production patterns;

(b)	The formulation and negotiation of trade policies by countries and implementation and administration of trade agreements at the bilateral, regional, plurilateral and multilateral levels, while ensuring special and differentiated treatment for developing countries;
(c)	More effective participation by countries in the region in global and regional value chains, including through reduced logistics overcost and increased sustainable trade.
Programme performance in 2025
A regional input-output table to inform evidence-based policymaking in Latin America and the Caribbean

21.25	In 2025, the subprogramme contributed to the strengthening of evidence-based policymaking in Latin America and the Caribbean by advancing analytical tools to support a deeper understanding of regional integration, productive structures and exposure to external shocks. Within this context, the subprogramme launched the regional input-output table for Latin America and the Caribbean, which connects the productive structures of 18 countries (10 from South America, 6 from Central America, Mexico and the Dominican Republic). The regional input-output table provides an instrument to conduct trade analysis in value-added terms, assess productive structures and value chains and estimate economic impacts under different demand and supply scenarios, directly supporting more informed public policy design. The subprogramme also undertook the joint construction of a multi-country table in collaboration with the statistics team of the European Commission Joint Research Centre and Eurostat. This collaboration enabled the interconnection of Latin America and the Caribbean with the European Union, the United States of America and Asia and the Pacific, helping to expand the scope and relevance of regional economic analysis.
21.26	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 21.I).
Figure 21.I

The target was not met due to delays in planned activities, owing to a shortage of staff and limited resources to engage consultants stemming from the liquidity constraints, which led to delays in the preparation of diagnostics and the provision of advisory services to member States.

21.29	The subprogramme’s work contributed to two new policy proposals related to regional physical integration, infrastructure, transport and logistics, which did not meet the planned target of four for a total of eight new policy proposals. The target was not met due to delays in project implementation, owing specifically to a shortage of staff and limited resources stemming from liquidity constraints, which hampered the ability to travel to provide on-site advisory services.

Result 3: member States diversify their export basket through increased foreign sales of digitally delivered services – Proposed programme plan for 2027

21.31	Exports of digitally delivered services represent an opportunity for Latin America and the Caribbean to tap into fast-growing global demand for computer services, as well as finance, insurance, business and creative services. However, their contribution to total exports remains relatively small in many countries, owing in part to limited capabilities in areas such as generation of statistics, the availability of specialized teams to promote services exports and attract foreign direct investment (FDI), understanding of trade barriers regarding digital services and the role of talent development. In this context, over the past two years the subprogramme has included analysis and policy recommendations in its annual flagship publication, has published specific research on the topic and has delivered capacity-building activities to raise awareness and support Governments to promote the development of this dynamic export sector.
Lessons learned and planned change

21.32	The lesson for the subprogramme was that promoting digitally delivered services exports would benefit from a holistic approach to the provision of technical support to member States in areas such as FDI attraction, trade regulations for digital services, skills development, statistics and coordination mechanisms. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will provide a holistic approach to technical support to help member States to make progress in all key areas to increase exports of digitally delivered services.

Subprogramme 2 – Production and innovation
Objective

21.35	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to enhance the diversification and structural transformation of productive structures through investment, innovation and the diffusion of new technology and to strengthen links and networks between firms.
Strategy
21.36	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Promote policy advocacy and the exchange of experiences and best practices with respect to the dynamics of productive development policies, with a view to encouraging technological diversification and positive structural change among stakeholders at the regional, national and subnational levels through meetings, seminars and virtual forums, including the provision of technical cooperation services, such as specific training courses and workshops;

(b)	Focus on the integration of innovation in production processes, including new low-carbon and low-emission technologies, with an emphasis on technology convergence and on mainstreaming a gender perspective on structural transformation;

(c)	Conduct economic analyses and research based on the generation, processing and analysis of information and indicators, disseminate the results of those studies and submit policy recommendations to governments, regional bodies and other relevant stakeholders;

(d)	Facilitate the connection of micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises with larger firms that use advanced technologies through cluster initiatives to integrate them into the process of learning and competition in foreign and domestic markets and increase the dissemination of technology in dynamic sectors that ECLAC identifies as drivers of productivity and employment growth;

(e)	Analyse and measure the impact of FDI to devise productive development policies that will have positive effects on the upgrading, diversification and positive structural change of production and export structures;

(f)	Provide technical assistance, upon request, on the assessment, development and implementation of productive development policies and strategies, thus mainly contributing to the attainment of Sustainable Development Goals 8, 9, 12 and 17;

(g)	Continue to provide analysis of firms and sectors in the region and further support member States in mitigating fragilities and imbalances in the productive structure, with specific attention given to the digitalization of the productive sector;

(h)	Conduct analyses and applied research on issues relevant to productive upgrading, diversification and positive structural change and the sustainability and social inclusiveness of agriculture and bioeconomy development;

(i)	Promote the coherence, integration and coordination of national, subnational and regional policies and institutions in agricultural development and provide technical assistance on issues related to sustainable agriculture and bioeconomy development.
21.37	These workstreams will help member States to make progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals, with an emphasis on Goals 2, 8, 9, 12, 13, 14, 15 and 17.
21.38	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	The design and implementation of productive development policies, including technology policies, by governments and regulators aimed at upgrading, diversifying and generating a positive structural change in the productive structure and building capacities in Latin American and Caribbean countries;
(b)	The incorporation of innovation and convergent technologies into production processes;
(c)	The design and implementation of policies by governments and regulators that heighten local innovation and adaptation, helping to leverage the potential of FDI;
(d)	Member States moving towards more sustainable patterns of consumption and production;
(e)	An upgrading of the diversification process associated with the creation of new opportunities for formal jobs and productivity growth;
(f)	Increased resilience of the productive sectors through a significant increase in the digitalization of firms, including micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises;

(g)	The incorporation of new concepts and technologies into the design and implementation of evidence-based bioeconomy- and digitization-related policies and strategies for sustainable and inclusive agriculture, food systems and rural development;

(h)	The design and implementation of productive development policies, including technology policies, in strategic and dynamizing sectors, including agriculture, the bioeconomy, modern manufacturing and services, aimed at upgrading, diversifying and generating a positive structural change in the productive structure, including increasing value addition.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened integration of a gender perspective into territorial productive development policies

21.39	Gender gaps are associated with challenges to inclusive and sustainable productive development, notably in value chains at the territorial level, with persistent occupational segregation and where women’s participation in decision-making roles remains limited. The subprogramme provided technical assistance to the government of Canelones, Uruguay, to strengthen the integration of a gender perspective into productive development policies, using the viticultural and vinicultural value chain as a pilot case. The subprogramme supported this process through analytical work, stakeholder engagement and capacity-building activities, including the development of a conceptual framework on gender-sensitive productive development policies, a gender analysis of the selected value chain and a set of policy recommendations aimed at addressing barriers to women’s full, equal and meaningful participation in territorial productive development. The subprogramme’s activities also facilitated dialogue and coordination with subnational authorities, contributing to the systematization of institutional interactions and supporting the translation of analytical findings into actionable policy inputs. As a result, the government strengthened its institutional capacities to integrate a gender perspective into productive development strategies and to advance coordinated actions across relevant departments.

Result 3: productive development policies that foster productive sophistication, diversification and structural change for equality – Proposed programme plan for 2027

21.45	The subprogramme has been providing technical support to member States in the design and implementation of productive development policies aimed at fostering productive sophistication, diversification and structural change for equality. Building on its analytical work and policy engagement, the subprogramme has been supporting countries in strengthening frameworks that promote higher-value-added activities, innovation and stronger productive linkages across sectors and territories.
Lessons learned and planned change

21.46	The lesson for the subprogramme was that interventions can have greater impact when they are embedded in coherent frameworks oriented towards diversification, technological upgrading and equality and supported by adequate institutional coordination. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will emphasize the need for integrated policy designs and strengthened inter-institutional coordination to reinforce institutional capacities and foster evidence-based policymaking in order to enhance structural change and productivity growth. Through advisory services, technical assistance and capacity-building, the subprogramme will contribute to integrated public policies that align industrial, innovation, trade and productivity objectives, supporting strategies to overcome structural constraints and promote inclusive and sustainable growth.

Subprogramme 3 – Macroeconomic policies and growth
Objective

21.49	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to foster sustainable and inclusive growth in Latin America and the Caribbean by strengthening the capacity of policymakers and other stakeholders in the region to analyse current and emerging macroeconomic and development finance issues and to evaluate, design and implement development-centred macroeconomic and development financing policies that incorporate a gender perspective and are based on comparative policy analysis.
Strategy
21.50	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Prepare the annual Economic Survey of Latin America and the Caribbean, the Preliminary Overview of the Economies of Latin America and the Caribbean, the Fiscal Panorama of Latin America and the Caribbean and other reports focusing on Latin America and the Caribbean to support member States in responding to current and emerging macroeconomic and financing for development trends;

(b)	Conduct applied analysis to support member States in the evaluation and formulation of macroeconomic and financing for development policies that promote equitable economic growth in line with the Sustainable Development Goals;

(c)	Facilitate South-South interaction of policymakers and dialogue with regional and international organizations through seminars, including the annual Regional Seminar on Fiscal Policy, and through workshops on macroeconomic and financing for development policies;

(d)	Foster the establishment and operation of networks of key stakeholders, including policymakers, academics, private sector analysts, members of civil society organizations and the public at large, to enable regional discussion of key macroeconomic and financing for development issues;
(e)	Provide technical cooperation and advisory services, upon request, in the areas of macroeconomic, labour and financing for development policies.
21.51	These workstreams will help member States to make progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals, with an emphasis on Goals 8, 16 and 17.
21.52	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced analysis of current and emerging macroeconomic and development finance issues by policymakers and other stakeholders in the region;

(b)	Strengthened evaluation, design and implementation by policymakers of macroeconomic and development financing policies that mainstream a gender perspective and are based on comparative policy analysis.
Programme performance in 2025
Costa Rica advances the development of social impact bonds to strengthen domestic resource mobilization

21.53	Accelerating progress towards the Sustainable Development Goals in Latin America and the Caribbean requires the strengthening of domestic public resource mobilization. Social impact bonds can be an innovative and impactful tool for mobilizing resources to finance catalytic social and economic investments. The subprogramme has worked to position social impact bonds within the regional debate on financing for development by providing comparative applied policy analysis and disseminating findings on their potential role in supporting financing for development through key regional forums, including the community of practice for development banks created by ECLAC, the Latin American Association of Development Financing Institutions and the Inter-American Development Bank. In this context, and upon request, the subprogramme delivered technical support to Costa Rica for the development of a social impact bond framework.

21.57	The subprogramme’s work contributed to one additional country, Costa Rica, estimating and evaluating tax expenditure in line with the objectives of the Sustainable Development Goals, which met the planned target.

Result 3: expanded domestic public resource mobilization in line with the objectives of the Sevilla Commitment – Proposed programme plan for 2027

21.59	To help to mobilize additional resources to advance the implementation of the 2030 Agenda while preserving fiscal sustainability, the Sevilla Commitment identifies avenues to expand fiscal space, including strengthening progressive taxation, addressing tax evasion, reviewing tax expenditure and leveraging national development banks. The subprogramme supports countries by building capacities to evaluate and formulate resource mobilization policies. It also serves as the technical secretariat for the Regional Platform for Tax Cooperation in Latin America and the Caribbean and promotes South-South cooperation through the Regional Seminar on Fiscal Policy and technical working groups. Its policy analysis and macroeconomic monitoring also inform policy design and evaluation.
Lessons learned and planned change

21.60	The lesson for the subprogramme was that countries in the region are at different stages of strengthening domestic public resources. The technical working groups helped to facilitate the exchange of experiences and tools, leading several countries to adopt new practices, including methods to identify and estimate environmentally related tax expenditure. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will intensify efforts to identify high-impact opportunities for South-South collaboration and to target exchanges towards policymakers best positioned to implement reforms.

Subprogramme 4 – Social development and equality
Objective

21.63	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to improve the overall well-being of the people of the region and achieve greater social and economic equality in line with the 2030 Agenda and with full respect for human rights.
Strategy

21.64	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will focus on addressing the multiple dimensions of poverty, inequality and well-being; bridging gaps in well-being and addressing inequalities; ensuring access to universal, comprehensive and sustainable social protection; promoting social and labour inclusion policies; promoting health, pensions and educational policies as key elements of inclusive social development; and improving the capacity of social policy to adapt to new and emerging challenges related to topics such as demographic transition, migration, changes in the labour market, new technologies and climate change. In implementing these priorities, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Support countries in implementing the Regional Agenda for Inclusive Social Development, in line with the outcomes and agreements reached at the Regional Conference on Social Development in Latin America and the Caribbean, by conducting applied research, generating analyses, disseminating results, providing training and advisory services, and formulating policy recommendations, as well as by promoting policy dialogue for inclusive social development among policymakers, academia, civil society organizations and other stakeholders, and by facilitating the exchange of experiences and good practices;

(b)	Assist countries in the design, implementation, monitoring and evaluation of strategies, policies and programmes. Research and advisory services will emphasize social protection systems with full respect for human rights, taking an equality-oriented and sustainable approach; the protection and promotion of the rights of vulnerable groups; social investment; and education and public health systems;
(c)	Assist governments in addressing the socioeconomic consequences of crises through advisory services and knowledge-sharing activities in affected social policy areas.
21.65	These workstreams will help member States to make progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals, with an emphasis on Goals 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 8, 10 and 16.
21.66	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Deeper understanding by policymakers of the structural social challenges and disparities that characterize the region;
(b)	Measures and public policies promoting greater social and economic equality and overall well-being of the people of the region;

(c)	Expanded and improved social protection systems that take into consideration the social impacts that arise from crises, emerging challenges and catastrophic events and the need to build greater resilience over time.
Programme performance in 2025
Paraguay establishes the social registry of households, helping to strengthen its social protection system

21.67	In April 2024, the Ministry of Social Development of Paraguay requested technical support from the subprogramme to strengthen its social registries. The subprogramme conducted technical workshops, high-level events and capacity-building activities and prepared various technical materials. ECLAC recommendations regarding strengthening the regulatory framework and the institutional capacity of these registries within the country’s social protection system were endorsed by national authorities.

children.text: Result 3: design of intersectoral policy instruments for digital inclusion and transformation aimed at reducing inequality – Proposed programme plan for 2027

21.73	Rapid digital transformation underscores the importance of digital inclusion to reduce inequalities, as those without access to digital tools and education risk being left behind. The subprogramme has made progress in promoting digital social protection and the strengthening of social registries, and in measuring digital skills and opportunities for children in the digital age. Proactive policies that anticipate technological shifts can help to safeguard the social safety net and promote equitable access to benefits.
Lessons learned and planned change

21.74	The lesson for the subprogramme was that there is growing demand from countries to integrate the digital dimension across areas of social development and that digital inclusion can be suitably addressed through an intersectoral approach. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will develop knowledge products with policy recommendations and promote spaces for advancing technical discussions among member States on the design of strategies and policies that incorporate digital aspects.

Subprogramme 5 – Gender equality and women’s autonomy
Objective
21.77	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to strengthen gender equality and women’s autonomy in sustainable development strategies of the Latin American and Caribbean countries.
Strategy
21.78	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Produce knowledge and develop gender statistics and indicators, expanding the scope and improving the quality of data and indicators available from the Gender Equality Observatory for Latin America and the Caribbean;
(b)	Provide technical support to mainstream a gender perspective into the work of ECLAC subsidiary bodies;

(c)	Provide technical assistance to the countries in the region in developing policies to promote gender equality and women’s autonomy, and strengthen the capacities of national and subnational governments (mechanisms for the advancement of women, among other stakeholders);

(d)	Foster the increased dissemination of publications and research findings among policymakers and other relevant stakeholders, including through the Gender Equality Observatory for Latin America and the Caribbean. Specific attention will be devoted to the economic autonomy of women in an integrated framework, connected to physical autonomy and to decision-making autonomy.
21.79	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced production of gender statistics by national statistical offices;
(b)	Strengthened capacities of member States in building policies for gender equality along with the implementation of the 2030 Agenda;
(c)	Advancement of gender equality across the region.
Programme performance in 2025

21.80	The subprogramme provided analysis, technical assistance and policy recommendations to help to shape the Tlatelolco Commitment, which sets a decade of action (2025–2035) to advance a care society through governance reforms, sustainable finance and inclusive dialogue.

children.text: Result 3: countries calculate the impact of investing in care policies – Proposed programme plan for 2027

21.86	Care work can support social well-being, economic development and gender equality, yet it is disproportionately borne by women. As ageing and rising care demands intensify, public care services are an important policy lever for achieving gender equality. The subprogramme, in partnership with the International Labour Organization (ILO) and national stakeholders, is implementing a “simulator”, an online tool developed by ILO that ECLAC is tailoring to the region to provide data and analysis on the financial requirements for care policy investments and on the impacts on the labour force of care policies. To advance this work, ECLAC and ILO established a working group with stakeholders in Brazil (unions, the private sector and the Government).
Lessons learned and planned change

21.87	The lesson for the subprogramme was the importance of tailoring a global tool to the national context in countries where the online tool will be used, as shown in Brazil. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will engage policymakers to define scenarios and parameters based on national legislation and policies, enhancing the policy relevance of future assessments and continuing collaboration with ILO.

children.text: Subprogramme 6 – Population and development
Objective

21.90	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to fully integrate population issues into development planning, policies and programmes of the countries of Latin America and the Caribbean.
Strategy
21.91	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide training on demographic analysis, population estimates and projections; and generate knowledge on population and demographic trends to underpin evidence-based policymaking and improve the use of census data, vital statistics, administrative records and surveys through the development of procedures, computer programmes and information systems;

(b)	Provide technical support in the follow-up to international agreements, including the Framework of Action for the follow-up to the Programme of Action of the International Conference on Population and Development beyond 2014, the Montevideo Consensus on Population and Development, the Madrid International Plan of Action on Ageing, 2002, and the Global Compact for Safe, Orderly and Regular Migration, with gender-sensitive, disability and ethnic analysis, and in the follow-up of the population-related targets of the 2030 Agenda;

(c)	Provide technical assistance in the planning, design, implementation and dissemination of population and housing censuses and in the inclusion of a sociodemographic perspective in the design and implementation of policies at the national and local levels, and organize workshops and seminars to facilitate South-South cooperation, networking and the sharing of best practices;

(d)	Conduct and disseminate applied research and analysis on issues such as population ageing, fertility, persons with disabilities, international migration, Indigenous Peoples, Afrodescendent populations and the socioeconomic impact of demographic transition, including recommendations on how to reduce inequalities;
(e)	Play a leading role for the region in the United Nations Network on Migration and in other networks relevant to population issues.
21.92	These workstreams will help member States to make progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals, with an emphasis on Goals 1, 3, 5, 8, 10, 11 and 17.
21.93	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced monitoring of population trends by local and national authorities;
(b)	Increased use of demographic analysis, population estimates and other evidence for policymaking.
Programme performance in 2025
Plurinational State of Bolivia publishes the results of its population and housing census

21.94	Census data at the national and subnational levels help to underpin analysis by the public and private sectors, as well as academia, and support evidence-based policymaking. In collaboration with the United Nations Population Fund, the subprogramme has been providing technical support to the National Statistical Office of the Plurinational State of Bolivia to strengthen national capacities to conduct the 2024 population and housing census and has participated in the High-level International Commission for the census of the Plurinational State of Bolivia. In August 2025, the census results were launched through public presentation seminars in La Paz and Santa Cruz, and census data were made available through the National Statistical Office website and geoportal, and through access to census microdata in Retrieval of Data for Small Areas by Microcomputer (REDATAM) format, providing updated information on national and subnational demographics, updating the last available information, which corresponded to the previous population and housing census, carried out in 2012.

children.text: Result 3: countries produce their own updated population and labour force estimates and projections – Proposed programme plan for 2027

21.100	Internationally comparable population data help in the monitoring of progress towards the Sustainable Development Goals and can foster better policymaking. Since 2017, the subprogramme has contributed to the production of the United Nations population estimates and projections for 20 Latin American countries, published in the World Population Prospects, as well as to the production of estimates and projections of labour force participation by age, sex and urban and rural areas.
Lessons learned and planned change

21.101	The lesson for the subprogramme was that further efficiencies can be achieved when countries apply the methodology used by ECLAC and use their own data to estimate and project population and labour force data, as doing so can facilitate the replicability of those estimates and projections. In applying the lesson, in 2027 the subprogramme will develop and publish working files (scripts and databases) that will be adaptable by each country to their own specific requirements and data availability and, as needed, will provide technical support to countries in the processing and validation of disaggregated population and labour force estimates and projections.

children.text: Subprogramme 7 – Sustainable development and human settlements
Objective

21.104	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure the integration and due consideration of environmental, climate and urban management concerns and opportunities into policymaking and policy implementation, with a rights-based approach and ensuring that no one is left behind.
Strategy
21.105	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Enhance knowledge of the region’s economic, social and environmental profiles and continue to convene and involve national and subnational governments, academia, civil society and other stakeholders to foster participatory decision-making;

(b)	Assess the advances made by countries in integrating sustainability criteria into public policies, including monitoring the implementation of principle 10 of the Rio Declaration on Environment and Development, as established in the Regional Agreement on Access to Information, Public Participation and Justice in Environmental Matters in Latin America and the Caribbean (Escazú Agreement), and providing support to the Forum of the Countries of Latin America and the Caribbean on Sustainable Development;

(c)	Strengthen national capacities to design and implement public policies for the sustainability of human settlements and the implementation of the 2030 Agenda, the New Urban Agenda, the nationally determined contributions under the Paris Agreement and the decisions taken within the framework of the Conference of the Parties to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change;

(d)	Promote the creation of networks with a wide range of stakeholders in the environmental, economic and social sectors relevant to sustainable development, including government institutions, academia, civil society, the private sector and relevant sectoral bodies;
(e)	Support the formulation of public policies to transform the development model, with a focus on driving sustainable growth and strengthening environmental institutions and governance;

(f)	Conduct the above-mentioned activities through research, knowledge products, the organization of expert group meetings, seminars and workshops, and the provision of technical assistance to member States, upon request, helping member States to make progress towards the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals 11, 12, 13, 15 and 16.
21.106	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Better-designed policies for sustainable development and environmental performance, policies to address the economics of climate change and policies that support sustainable and inclusive human settlements;
(b)	Sustainable development and climate change criteria effectively mainstreamed into more areas of government;
(c)	The design of guidelines for the implementation of economic plans towards a low-carbon, low-emission and more resilient economy that creates more employment and economic well-being.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened environmental impact assessment systems in Latin America

21.107	Environmental assessments can help provide an enabling environment for sustainable investments in the region. The subprogramme supported the Latin American Network of Environmental Impact Assessment Systems through the provision of technical assistance, the delivery of training courses and the preparation of a joint publication on the cumulative and synergistic environmental impacts of investment projects. These actions contributed to the establishment of thematic working groups and the development of common guidelines and tools to address various aspects of environmental licensing, including the strengthening of environmental certification processes, early public participation with a gender-sensitive approach, the integration of climate change considerations and the assessment of cumulative and synergistic impacts in the environmental impact assessment of investment projects.

children.text: Result 3: development of national priorities based on a common framework for implementation of the Escazú Agreement – Proposed programme plan for 2027

21.113	National authorities in the region increasingly request technical support to strengthen capacities to implement the Escazú Agreement, in accordance with their national priorities. The subprogramme has actively contributed to the development of road maps for the implementation of access rights in environmental matters in Latin America and the Caribbean in the States Parties to the Escazú Agreement.
Lessons learned and planned change

21.114	The lesson for the subprogramme was that to achieve greater impact and enhance efficiency in the implementation of the Agreement, it is helpful to establish national priorities based on a common methodology that facilitates coordination among stakeholders, generates synergies among countries and helps channel opportunities for regional and international cooperation. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will contribute to the application of a common framework for defining implementation priorities and will provide technical assistance and capacity-building support to the States Parties to the Escazú Agreement in this regard.

Subprogramme 8 – Natural resources
Objective

21.117	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to improve the governance and enhance the sustainable use and exploitation of natural resources in Latin America and the Caribbean, focusing on water resources management, affordable, inclusive and clean energy, extractive resources efficiency and biodiversity.
Strategy
21.118	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Support countries of the region in the design of policies related to the energy transition, including greater use of renewable sources of energy and increased energy efficiency, and to the water sustainable management transition, helping member States to make progress towards the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals 6 and 7;

(b)	Carry out studies on the governance of fossil and mineral resources, taking into consideration issues and challenges relating to the collection and use of resource rents, material efficiency and decoupling, helping member States to make progress towards the achievement of Goal 12;

(c)	Provide technical assistance and support multi-stakeholder dialogue, within the framework of more sustainable governance of the extractive industries, focusing on critical minerals for the energy transition to make progress towards the achievement of Goal 13;

(d)	Reinforce the coherence, integration and coordination of national and regional biodiversity policies and provide technical assistance, upon request, to member States on issues related to conservation and the sustainable use of biodiversity, helping member States to make progress towards the achievement of Goals 14 and 15.
21.119	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Improved management of clean and affordable energy and water;
(b)	Better conservation and sustainable use of biodiversity;
(c)	More sustainable extractive industries.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened political dialogue and regional governance in the mining sector

21.120	The Conference of Mining Ministries of the Americas is a ministerial forum for discussing mining sector policies, sharing experiences, strengthening coordination among Governments and identifying opportunities for bilateral and regional cooperation that drive a productive and sustainable transformation in the region. The subprogramme provided technical support to the Conference in organizing its fifteenth meeting, a high-level, multisectoral dialogue, with the participation of authorities from 13 Latin American and Caribbean countries, plus Canada. As a result, the authorities approved new terms of reference for the Conference, strengthening its institutional framework, and agreed that ECLAC would assume the role of its technical secretariat. Together with the Conference’s coordinating committee, a biennial action plan is being prepared based on the themes prioritized at the meeting: productive development of the mining sector with added value, critical minerals, territorial development in mining, governance of mining operations and strengthening technical, operational and technological capacities in the mining sector.

Result 3: strengthened ocean governance and policies to strengthen the sustainable use and management of the ocean in the region – Proposed programme plan for 2027

21.126	Countries of the region have opportunities to further strengthen ocean governance, including by enhancing regulatory frameworks, deepening the integration of marine biodiversity into national strategies and aligning fisheries subsidies with sustainability objectives. The subprogramme has been working to strengthen institutional capacities and governance to improve the design, adoption and implementation of sustainable ocean policies, as well as to enhance regional integration and policy coherence. In 2025, the subprogramme prepared an outlook report on the ocean, seas, and marine resources and their contribution to sustainable development in Latin America and the Caribbean and other publications to promote the sustainable blue bioeconomy in conserving, restoring and sustainably using the ocean.
Lessons learned and planned change

21.127	The lesson for the subprogramme was that pairing regional information products with actively promoting the adoption and improvement of sustainable ocean management policies in coordination with member States can help activate decision makers who can influence policymaking processes. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will foster closer collaboration with member States, facilitate regional dialogues to build a shared vision for the ocean and work actively with regional multilateral oceanic bodies.

Subprogramme 9 – Planning and public management for development
Objective
21.130	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to enhance planning and public management processes in the region for the advancement of equitable and sustainable development.
Strategy
21.131	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Promote the application of new methodologies, instruments and conceptual frameworks to strengthen national capacities in the entire cycle of public management, including foresight, strategic and territorial planning, digital and open government, and the monitoring and evaluation of plans and policies for participatory planning and public management, while fostering public investment and links between development and sector-based plans and budgets;

(b)	Encourage cooperation, peer-to-peer learning, innovation, and the sharing of experiences and good practices in planning and public management through the provision of technical cooperation services and training, as well as technical support to countries in the region, with a focus on building their technical, operational, political and prospective institutional capacities, and by conducting applied research in areas such as increasing public sector workforce diversity and the use of artificial intelligence;

(c)	Enhance regional capacities for building institutional resilience to cope with challenges posed by internal and external crises produced by natural disasters and/or human activities, such as pandemics, climate change impacts, social and political disruption and economic shocks.
21.132	These workstreams will help member States to make progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals, with an emphasis on Goals 5, 11, 13, 16 and 17.
21.133	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Effective, inclusive, smart and strategic institutions and planning processes that prioritize comprehensive development through medium- and long-term visions;
(b)	Reduced structural gaps by enhancing citizens’ participation, deliberation and accountability, and the strengthening of territorial governance, planning and management in public policy processes;

(c)	Strengthened capacities in Latin American and Caribbean institutions to withstand deep disruption and ensure the continuity of basic services while enhancing preparedness and responsive, adaptive and transformational institutional capacities, including digital solutions, at both the national and subnational levels.
Programme performance in 2025
Adoption of the Regional Agenda on Governance of Planning and Public Management for Sustainable Development in Latin America and the Caribbean to guide long-term strategic planning

21.134	The Regional Council for Planning of the Latin American and Caribbean Institute for Economic and Social Planning, a subsidiary body of ECLAC, played an enabling role in promoting a broad, multi-stakeholder dialogue that helped identify regional progress, gaps and shared challenges. As a result of this collective and deliberative process – bringing together national planning authorities, academic experts and other key actors – the Regional Agenda on Governance of Planning and Public Management for Sustainable Development in Latin America and the Caribbean was adopted at the twentieth meeting of the Regional Council, held in October 2025 in Brazil. The agenda represents a substantive milestone and a collective commitment to guide long-term strategic planning, strengthen institutional capacities and consolidate more resilient, forward-looking and socially legitimate governance frameworks across the region, integrating four strategic areas: collaborative governance, foresight, coordination and evaluation. The new agenda will help align long-term planning instruments across different levels of government, support context-specific adaptations and foster more coherent, anticipatory and results-oriented development strategies throughout the region.

Result 3: member States develop long-term national and subnational development plans that integrate the four strategic areas of the Regional Agenda on Governance of Planning and Public Management for Sustainable Development in Latin America and the Caribbean – Proposed programme plan for 2027

21.140	In a context of growing global uncertainty, accelerated transformations and increasing social demands, in Latin American and Caribbean countries the rejuvenation of planning and public management as core strategic functions of the State is increasingly relevant. Over the past 60 years, the Latin American and Caribbean Institute for Economic and Social Planning has accompanied member States in strengthening these functions. This long-standing experience has demonstrated both the value of planning and the need for its continuous updating to respond to new development challenges and complex governance environments, and to advance sustainable development. Building on this trajectory, the subprogramme has supported countries in rethinking planning and public management beyond their traditional technical scope. It has promoted a renewed vision that integrates collaborative governance, foresight, intersectoral coordination and evaluation focused on public value creation, which are the four areas of the Regional Agenda on Governance of Planning and Public Management for Sustainable Development in Latin America and the Caribbean.
Lessons learned and planned change

21.141	The lesson for the subprogramme was that long-term strategic planning and governance reforms are significantly strengthened through sustained multi-stakeholder dialogue and cumulative institutional learning. The process towards the adoption, in October 2025, of the Regional Agenda on Governance of Planning and Public Management for Sustainable Development in Latin America and the Caribbean at the twentieth meeting of the Regional Council for Planning of the Latin American and Caribbean Institute for Economic and Social Planning demonstrated that inclusive and iterative knowledge-building enhances the legitimacy, coherence and relevance of long-term governance frameworks. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will continue to strengthen participatory approaches, knowledge-sharing and technical cooperation to support member States in translating the Regional Agenda into actionable national and subnational long-term development strategies. Greater emphasis will be placed on facilitating dialogue across sectors and levels of government, promoting learning communities and fostering adaptive, future-oriented governance capacities.

Subprogramme 10 – Statistics
Objective
21.144	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to improve the production, dissemination and use of statistics for evidence-based decision-making in the region.
Strategy
21.145	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide technical assistance, training and capacity-building to strengthen the development of official statistics and institutional coordination mechanisms, including in national accounts and economic and price statistics; environmental, climate change and disaster risk reduction statistics; monetary poverty and multidimensional poverty, as well as inequality measurement; household survey design and the integration of data sources; integration of statistical and geospatial information systems; digital platforms and data portals for official statistics; follow-up and review of the Sustainable Development Goals; measures of progress that go beyond GDP; and data governance frameworks;

(b)	Compile and harmonize statistical indicators and geospatial data from selected primary data sources, including household surveys, economic surveys and non-traditional data sources, to produce regional economic, environmental and social statistics;

(c)	Develop and maintain the ECLAC statistical data portal (CEPALSTAT), the geospatial data portal (CEPALGEO), the regional household survey data bank (BADEHOG) and other regional statistical dissemination platforms, as well as the Statistical Yearbook for Latin America and the Caribbean and other publications;

(d)	Provide secretariat services to the Statistical Conference of the Americas, its working groups and the Regional Committee on United Nations Global Geospatial Information Management for the Americas;

(e)	Promote the use of administrative records and non-traditional data sources, their integration with traditional sources, and the responsible adoption and governance of artificial intelligence in statistical systems.
21.146	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Production of reliable economic, social and environmental statistics, geospatial data and new indicators in emerging areas and the improvement of non-traditional sources of information;
(b)	Increased availability of regionally comparable data, required as a benchmark for regional statistical development;
(c)	Greater regional coordination, leading to increased use of statistics at the regional and national levels.
Programme performance in 2025
Improved accuracy of data to inform policies on poverty, inequality, employment, education and living conditions

21.147	Household surveys constitute a primary source of social, demographic and economic statistics. Their statistical precision is linked to the availability of an up-to-date sampling frame, namely a comprehensive mapping of households in the national territory. The recent release of microdata from population censuses has enabled the development of revised and expanded sampling frames. The subprogramme has provided technical assistance to national statistical offices in constructing these updated frames, including capacity-building on emerging methodological approaches and guidance for addressing operational and technical challenges. By having made updated sampling frames available, member States are better equipped to produce relevant and more accurate statistics on poverty, inequality, employment, education and overall living conditions.

Result 3: modernized national statistical systems for measuring economic activity in Latin American and Caribbean countries – Proposed programme plan for 2027

21.153	Countries of the region are preparing for the transition to the System of National Accounts 2025, which reflects the growing relevance of digitalization, economic interlinkages and environmental dependencies. As part of this process, countries may need to update institutional arrangements, strengthen statistical infrastructure and integrate new data sources. The subprogramme has been supporting countries in addressing these challenges through technical assistance, methodological guidance and regional training activities.
Lessons learned and planned change

21.154	The lesson for the subprogramme was the benefit of moving beyond ad hoc technical assistance towards a more structured approach to supporting the implementation of the System of National Accounts 2025. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will introduce more comprehensive and standardized readiness assessments that go beyond earlier diagnostic exercises, which will be jointly conducted with national authorities. The subprogramme will also support countries through regional technical workshops focused on innovative compilation approaches, updated classifications and data integration techniques, to modernize national accounts compilation in line with the System of National Accounts 2025.

Subprogramme 11 – Subregional activities in Central America, Cuba, the Dominican Republic, Haiti and Mexico
Objective

21.157	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to improve the formulation of evidence-based public policies in the economic, social and environmental fields in the countries in the subregion.
Strategy

21.158	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will support the implementation of national development agendas and strategic reforms for greater inclusive and sustainable development, based on increasing productivity and reducing structural gaps. Special emphasis will be placed on economic and social development, macroeconomics for development, international trade, productive development, regional integration, agriculture, energy and natural resources, and climate change, thus helping member States to make progress towards the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals 1, 2, 7, 8, 10, 13 and 17. Specifically, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Undertake analytical work to foster the generation, dissemination and implementation of innovative and sound approaches to address the subregion’s development challenges and build national and subregional capacities to formulate more integrated and coherent policies, taking into full consideration the different national contexts in the subregion;
(b)	Strengthen its multisectoral and interdisciplinary approach by developing quantitative and qualitative analytical models and generating sound empirical evidence, including statistical databases;
(c)	Provide training activities, advisory services and fellowships, as well as organize and promote forums in the areas of economic, social and environmental public policies.

21.159	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in the design, implementation and evaluation of effective public policies, including those focused on macroeconomics for development, productive development policies, social protection, reduction of structural gaps, trade and regional integration, energy and natural resources, and agriculture.
Programme performance in 2025
Countries strengthen value chain–based productive development

21.160	Productive development policies can be further strengthened to reduce fragmentation and improve alignment with value chain dynamics, increasing their impact on productivity, diversification and inclusion. Building on analytical work and technical support at the local level that enabled policy dialogue over successive years, the subprogramme supported participatory processes to upgrade value chains in multiple countries and across several sectors. Through targeted technical assistance and capacity-building activities, national and subnational institutions improved their ability to identify strategic value chains, diagnose constraints and design policy instruments aligned with sectoral and territorial priorities. This work translated analytical frameworks into operational capacities, enabling public institutions to move from diagnosis to implementation and to better support a productive, inclusive and sustainable transformation.

Result 3: improved public strategies for fostering productive development in specific subnational areas and sectors – Proposed programme plan for 2027

21.166	Strengthening productive development can underpin sustainable growth, enhancing a country’s capacity to generate quality employment and reduce structural gaps. In this context, the subprogramme has been supporting national authorities in designing and implementing sectoral productive development strategies in several sectors, contributing to more coherent, evidence-based public policymaking at the national level.
Lessons learned and planned change

21.167	The lesson for the subprogramme was that, alongside the indispensable role of regional-level and national-level analysis and dialogue, more direct engagement with subnational governments across multiple sectors can facilitate meaningful policy uptake, as territorial perspectives and sector-specific approaches are essential to designing policies that respond to local development dynamics. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will place greater emphasis on supporting subnational authorities through multisectoral, territory-based technical assistance, complementing regional knowledge products with tailored guidance that strengthens value-chain development and improves coherence between national and local strategies.

Subprogramme 12 – Subregional activities in the Caribbean
Objective

21.170	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to strengthen the sustainable development process in the Caribbean in the economic, social and environmental dimensions, and to enhance the subregion’s cooperation with Latin America.
Strategy
21.171	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Engage member States, regional and extraregional entities and other partners through close cooperation with ECLAC substantive divisions, the ECLAC subregional office in Mexico and national offices, in line with the Commission’s “Caribbean first” approach, by implementing subregional and country-specific initiatives, convening intergovernmental and technical meetings in support of the follow-up to global and regional agendas, including the 2030 Agenda and the Antigua and Barbuda Agenda for Small Island Developing States;
(b)	Support Caribbean countries in the development of evidence-based policies that promote sustainable development;

(c)	Undertake research and analysis and advance strategies for development and disaster risk management, paying particular attention to emerging issues and challenges facing the Caribbean, and strengthen their visibility and policy relevance;

(d)	Build capacities of development practitioners and decision makers in designing and implementing resilience-building measures for sustainable development that are inclusive of women and persons with disabilities, in close cooperation with United Nations entities and Caribbean development partners;
(e)	Support the Caribbean Knowledge Management Centre with specialized reference and information services for member States and stakeholders.
21.172	These workstreams will help member States to make progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals, with an emphasis on Goals 4, 5, 10, 12, 15 and 17.
21.173	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Increased effective subregional and regional engagement and consensus, cooperation and enhanced political advocacy on issues critical to the development of the Caribbean;
(b)	Advancement of the implementation of national and subregional strategies and programmes.
Programme performance in 2025
Development of an integrative natural resources management approach for sargassum management in the Caribbean

21.174	Since 2011, large-scale sargassum seaweed influxes have posed significant environmental and socioeconomic challenges to Caribbean small island development States, posing a threat to tourism, fisheries, marine transportation, ecosystems, water salination and public health. In 2023, the Caribbean Development and Cooperation Committee specifically identified sargassum as a climate change-exacerbated threat. The subprogramme conducted data collection and research, which proposed an integrative natural resources management approach for sargassum management, incorporating case studies from Barbados, Saint Lucia, Trinidad and Tobago, and Guadeloupe, alongside insights, best practices and lessons on effective and sustained management from regional bodies, tertiary research institutions and the private sector, which led to the development of the public-led model for the sustainable management of sargassum blooms.

children.text: Result 3: economic diversification through increased South-South cooperation with Latin American economies – Proposed programme plan for 2027

21.180	To overcome challenges such as dependence on international trade, small markets and structural unemployment, Caribbean economies can leverage South-South cooperation to expand beyond their traditional focus on limited sectors, thus promoting broader and more sustainable economic growth. In a recent high-level seminar convened by the subprogramme on South-South cooperation, key strategies for Caribbean development were identified, including enhancing collaboration with Latin America, prioritizing regional ownership and fostering financial innovation and digital transformation.
Lessons learned and planned change

21.181	The lesson for the subprogramme was the importance of engaging line ministers and permanent secretaries early to secure high-level buy-in. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will organize instances of coordination between the directors of trade, tourism and planning and other high-level government officials in each beneficiary country. This collaboration across national entities will support the formulation of policies that facilitate investment inflow and the development of key modern services subsectors which can support structural transformation in Caribbean countries.
